title: Maturity Indicators
description: Harvesting pomegranates at the correct stage of maturity is critical for optimal sweetness, aril colour, and shelf life. Both visual and physical indicators should be assessed together.
indicator: Harvest when fruit surface colour changes from green to yellow-bronze (variety dependent), the fruit emits metallic sound on tapping, and the calyx slightly closes.
tips: Fruit skin colour: Green → Yellow → Orange/Red depending on variety
TSS (Total Soluble Solids): 14–16°Brix is ideal for harvesting
Acidity drops below 1.5% at maturity
Seed colour: White seeds turn dark red/pink at full maturity
Days from fruit set: 120–180 days depending on variety and climate
Metallic sound on tapping indicates firm, mature fruit; hollow sound suggests over-maturity

title: Harvest Timing
description: In India, pomegranate has two main crop seasons: Ambe Bahar (flowering in January–February, harvest June–August) and Mrig Bahar (flowering June–July, harvest November–January).
indicator: Harvest in early morning or late evening to avoid heat stress on harvested fruit. Avoid harvesting when rain is expected within 24 hours.
tips: Ambe Bahar crop fetches higher price due to off-season availability
Mrig Bahar accounts for majority of commercial production
Hasth Bahar (September–October) suits areas with water scarcity in summer
Evening harvest reduces fruit temperature and improves storage life
Stagger harvest over 5–7 days as not all fruits ripen simultaneously

title: Harvesting Method
description: Proper harvesting technique prevents mechanical injury which leads to post-harvest quality loss, pathogen entry, and reduced shelf life.
indicator: Use sharp, sterilised pruning shears or secateurs to cut fruit with 1–2 cm of stalk attached. Never pull or twist the fruit off the branch — this damages the calyx and increases rot incidence.
tips: Sterilise pruning tools with 70% alcohol between cuts to prevent disease spread
Keep 1–2 cm of stalk on fruit to minimise calyx damage
Harvest into plastic crates lined with soft padding — avoid direct metal contact
Do not stack more than 2–3 layers of fruits in harvesting crates
Wear gloves to prevent skin oils and sweat affecting fruit skin bloom

title: Post-Harvest Handling
description: Post-harvest losses in pomegranate can reach 20–40% without proper handling. Quick cooling, gentle handling, and hygienic packing significantly extend shelf life and market value.
indicator: Sort fruits into 3 grades within 4 hours of harvest. Grade A: >350g, blemish-free. Grade B: 200–350g, minor blemishes. Grade C: <200g or cosmetically damaged.
tips: Pre-cool harvested fruit to 8–10°C within 4 hours of harvest
Hydro-cooling or forced-air cooling is most efficient for rapid temperature reduction
Wax coating (food-grade carnauba wax) reduces water loss and extends shelf life by 2–3 weeks
Pack in corrugated fibre board (CFB) boxes with newspaper cushioning
Standard export pack: 8–10 fruits per box (4–5 kg net weight)
Apply Carbendazim 0.1% wax treatment to prevent fruit rot during storage

title: Storage & Shelf Life
description: Controlled atmosphere and cold chain management are essential for export markets. Pomegranates can be stored for up to 3 months under optimal cold storage conditions.
indicator: Storage temperature: 5–8°C. Relative humidity: 90–95%. These conditions suppress fungal pathogens while maintaining fruit weight and TSS.
tips: Cold storage shelf life: up to 90 days at 5–8°C
Ambient storage shelf life: 15–20 days at 25°C
Controlled atmosphere (3% CO₂ + 2% O₂) extends shelf life by 2–4 weeks
Modified atmosphere packaging (MAP) bags reduce respiration rate
Check stored fruit every 7 days and remove any showing signs of decay
Do not store with ethylene-producing fruits (banana, apple) — accelerates ripening

title: Yield & Economics
description: Pomegranate becomes commercially productive from the 3rd–4th year after planting. Yields increase progressively, reaching maximum productivity at 8–10 years.
indicator: A well-managed mature orchard of Bhagwa variety should yield 30–40 kg/plant/year, translating to approximately 20–25 MT/hectare at standard spacing.
tips: Year 1–2: Establishment phase — no commercial yield
Year 3–4: First commercial crop — 8–12 kg/plant
Year 5–7: Increasing yield — 20–30 kg/plant
Year 8–15: Peak production — 30–45 kg/plant
Economic life of orchard: 20–25 years with proper management
Grade A fruits fetch ₹60–120/kg depending on season and market
